8.23: The regnal closing formula: wĕyeter diḇrê yôrām wĕkol-ʾăšer ʿāśāh hălem-hēm kĕtûḇîm ʿal-sēper diḇrê hayyāmîm lĕmalkê yĕhûḏāh — ‘the rest of the acts of Joram and everything he did, are they not written in the Book of the Annals of the Kings of Judah?’ This formula, repeated for almost every king, presupposes the reader’s access to other records — the Kings narrative is selective, not comprehensive. The historian has chosen which details serve the theological argument about covenant fidelity and its consequences.
8.24: wayyiškaḇ yôrām ʿim-ʾăḇōtāyw — ‘Joram rested with his ancestors.’ The standard death formula šākab ʿim ʾăḇōtāyw (lay down/slept with his fathers) is used across the Davidic line. The variation wayyiqqāḇēr ʾittām bĕʿîr dāwîḏ — ‘buried with them in the city of David’ — indicates continuation of the royal burial tradition, despite Joram’s apostasy.
8.25: bišnat šĕtêm ʿeśrēh šānāh lĕyôrām ben-ʾaḥʾāḇ melek yiśrāʾēl — the synchronistic formula anchors Ahaziah of Judah’s accession to year 12 of Israel’s Joram. The two northern and southern reigns are tracked in tandem throughout Kings — the historian’s method of maintaining chronological coherence across the divided monarchy.
8.26: Ahaziah’s regnal formula: ben-ʿeśrîm ûšĕtayim šānāh — twenty-two years old. Reigned one year (šānāh ʾaḥat mālaḵ bîrûšālaim). His mother: ʿătalyāhû bat-ʿomrî melek yiśrāʾēl, ‘Athaliah the daughter of Omri king of Israel.’ The Omride connection through the maternal line (even more proximate than through his father’s marriage to an Ahab daughter) marks Ahaziah as doubly contaminated by the house of Ahab.
8.27: wayyēlek bĕderek bêt ʾaḥʾāḇ — ‘he walked in the way of the house of Ahab.’ The phrase is used four times across Jehoram and Ahaziah (vv18, 27): the Ahab contamination of the Davidic line is the historian’s central concern in this section. The theological verdict: wayyaʿaś hārāʿ bĕʿênê YHWH kaʾăšer bêt ʾaḥʾāḇ — ‘he did evil in the sight of YHWH as the house of Ahab had done.’
8.28: Political narrative: Ahaziah went with Joram son of Ahab to fight against Hazael king of Syria at Ramoth-gilead. The verb wayyēlek... lammilḥāmāh — ‘went to war.’ The mention of Hazael confirms the fulfillment of Elisha’s oracle (vv13-15): Hazael is now king of Aram and attacking Israel. The narrative loop closes: Elisha prophesied Hazael’s kingship and atrocities; Hazael is now executing them.
8.29: Joram is wounded: wayyāšob yôrām hammelek lĕhitrappēʾ bĕyizrĕʿēl — ‘King Joram returned to Jezreel to recover from the wounds.’ The verb rāpāʾ (heal, be healed) — Joram retreats to Jezreel, the Omride winter capital and the site of Naboth’s vineyard. The chapter ends with Ahaziah going to Jezreel to visit (lirʾôt, ‘to see’) the wounded Joram — a visit that will lead to his own death in the Jehu narrative (ch 9). The narrative tension is established without resolution.

21.1: Manasseh was twelve years old at his accession and reigned fifty-five years — the longest reign in the Judean monarchy. The extreme length is theologically loaded: the Deuteronomistic History frames it as evidence that YHWH’s patience is not indifference. Ḥizqiyyāhû (Hezekiah) and Mᵉnaššeh (Manasseh) are placed in immediate succession: the son of the most faithful Judean king becomes the most faithless. The name Mᵉnaššeh means “one who causes to forget” — a name the narrative validates, as he forgets everything his father restored.
21.2: “He did what was evil in the sight of YHWH, following the tôʿăḇōt (detestable practices) of the nations YHWH had driven out.” The tôʿăḇôt (singular tôʿēḇāh) is the standard Deuteronomic/Levitical term for cultic abomination — actions structurally incompatible with covenant holiness (Deut 12:31; 18:9-12; Lev 18:22-30). The comparison is inverted from the usual formula: normally Manasseh is judged against David; here he is measured against the Canaanites whom YHWH expelled. “More evil than the nations YHWH had destroyed before Israel” (v9) will sharpen this reversal. The one who held covenant land must not replicate the practices that made the previous occupants forfeit it.
21.3: Manasseh “rebuilt the high places his father Hezekiah had demolished.” The verb bānāh (built/rebuilt) echoes ironically against Hezekiah’s hēsîr (removed) in 18:4. What Hezekiah tore down, Manasseh rebuilds; what Hezekiah threw out, Manasseh sets up. The pairing with “as Ahab king of Israel had done” is the Deuteronomistic History’s harshest condemnation of a Judean king: he is measured not against the bad kings of Judah but against the worst of the northern dynasty. The ʾăšērāh pole returns — the cult object of the fertility goddess Asherah, the consort of El/Baal in Canaanite religion, whose presence in Judah was a direct violation of the first two commandments.
21.4: “He built altars in the house of YHWH, of which YHWH had said, ‘In Jerusalem I will put my Name.’” The phrase “put my Name” (lāśûm šᵉmî) is the Deuteronomic formula for YHWH’s unique dwelling choice (Deut 12:5, 11, 21; 14:23-24; 1 Kgs 8:29). To erect pagan altars in the Name’s specific dwelling is not merely syncretism but a direct assault on the covenantal address-point of YHWH’s presence. The temple altars to Baal occupy the space consecrated to the Name.
21.5: “In both courts of the house of YHWH he built altars to all the starry hosts.” Ṣeḇāʾ haššāmayim — “host of heaven/starry hosts” — refers to the astral cult of Mesopotamian origin: worship of sun, moon, planets, and stars as deities or divine intermediaries. Deut 4:19 explicitly warns against being “drawn away to bow down to them and worship them.” Mesopotamian astral religion had penetrated the Levant through Assyrian cultural pressure during the 7th century; Manasseh’s reign (697-642 BCE) coincided with the height of Neo-Assyrian dominance, and his astral cult may reflect political as well as religious conformity to the overlord.
21.6: The catalogue of Manasseh’s abominations reaches its peak: “He sacrificed his son in the fire (heʿĕḇîr ʾet bᵉnô bāʾēš), practiced divination (ʿônēn), sought omens (niḥēš), and consulted mediums (ʾōḇôt) and spiritists (yiddᵉʿōnîm).” The verb heʿĕḇîr bāʾēš — “passed through fire” — is the standard idiom for child sacrifice to Molech (Lev 18:21; 20:2-5; Deut 18:10). ʿōnēn (cloud-reading/soothsaying), nāḥaš (divination by omens/serpents), ʾōḇ (medium/ghost-consulter), and yiddᵉʿōnî (spiritist/one with a familiar spirit) — this list precisely matches the prohibitions of Deut 18:10-11. Manasseh does not miss a single item on the Deuteronomic list of forbidden practices.
21.7: The carved Asherah pole (pesel hāʾăšērāh) is placed inside the temple itself — the holy of holies’ antechamber. The specific violation: Deut 16:21 explicitly prohibits planting an Asherah “beside the altar of YHWH your God.” The divine word quoted — “In this house and in Jerusalem, which I have chosen out of all the tribes of Israel, I will put my Name forever” — is the election formula at its most emphatic: “forever” (lᵉʿôlām). The permanence of YHWH’s election is the standard against which Manasseh’s desecration is measured. The idol stands in the place of the Name.
21.8: The conditional promise: “I will no longer make Israel’s feet wander from the land I gave their ancestors — only if they carefully observe everything I commanded them, all the law my servant Moses commanded.” The raq (“only if”) introduces the condition; šmr in the infinitive absolute construction (šāmᵉrû lāʿăśôt) — “diligently observing to do” — expresses the intensity required. This conditional promise is quoted at the moment of Manasseh’s maximum violation to make the contrast explicit: the promise existed; the condition was not met; the consequence will follow.
21.9: “But they did not listen, and Manasseh led them astray (wayyatʿēm) so that they did more evil than the nations YHWH had destroyed before Israel.” Tāʿāh (causative: to cause to err/wander) is the same root used of the false prophet who “leads you astray” in Deut 13:5. Manasseh functions as a false prophet in royal office — he is the king as the anti-Moses. The comparison to “the nations YHWH had destroyed” is the strongest possible indictment: Israel has descended below the covenant-free pagans who preceded them. Greater privilege = greater accountability (cf. Amos 3:2).
21.10: “The LORD said through his servants the prophets” — the plural marks that this is not an isolated oracle but the sustained prophetic witness of the period (Isaiah, Micah, and the unnamed prophets of the Assyrian era all preached in this context). The indirect delivery — the narrator summarizes rather than quoting individual oracles — is the Deuteronomistic History’s way of showing that the prophetic word was pervasive and unavoidable. The disaster that follows is not unforeseen; it was announced.
21.11: The prophetic indictment names three grounds: (1) ḥiqqûṣîm — “detestable acts” (from šāqaṣ, to abhor/detest — a stronger term than tôʿēḇāh); (2) “more evil than the Amorites before him” — the comparison restated; (3) “made Judah sin with his idols (gillûlîm).” Gillûlîm — a deliberately contemptuous term for idols, likely derived from gēl (dung-pellets) — is Ezekiel’s characteristic pejorative for pagan cult objects (used 39 times in Ezekiel). Its use here links Manasseh’s idols to the language of the exile prophets who interpreted the destruction Manasseh brought on.
21.12: “I am going to bring such disaster on Jerusalem and Judah that the ears of everyone who hears about it will tingle.” Wəṣilᵉlāh šᵉtê ʾoznāyw — “both his ears will tingle/ring.” The formula occurs in 1 Sam 3:11 (Eli’s house) and Jer 19:3 (Jerusalem’s destruction) — it marks an event so catastrophic that merely hearing about it produces a physical sensation of shock. The oracle announces what will become the Babylonian exile without yet naming the agent.
21.13: “I will stretch over Jerusalem the measuring line used against Samaria and the plumb line used against the house of Ahab.” Qaw šōmᵉrôn (Samaria’s measuring line) and mišqelet bêt ʾaḥʾāḇ (Ahab’s plumb line) are the architect’s instruments — used here as demolition tools. When a builder stretches a line and drops a plumb, he is measuring whether the wall is true; here YHWH is measuring Jerusalem against the same standard he applied to Samaria (destroyed 722 BCE) and Ahab’s house (destroyed within a generation). The image continues: “I will wipe Jerusalem as one wipes a dish, wiping it and turning it upside down” — the ordinary household act of cleaning becomes an image of total erasure.
21.14: “I will abandon the remnant of my inheritance (šᵉʾērît naḥălātî) and hand them over to their enemies.” Naḥălāh (inheritance/portion) is the covenantal term for Israel as YHWH’s share among the nations (Deut 4:20; 9:26, 29; 32:9). YHWH’s “abandoning” (nāṭaš) his inheritance is the sharpest possible reversal of the covenant: the God who “does not forsake his people” (1 Sam 12:22; Ps 94:14) announces that covenant forsaking has been so complete on Israel’s side that the relationship is structurally terminated. The šᵉʾērît (remnant) had been the language of hope; here it is the object of abandonment.
21.15: The reason for the abandonment is rooted in the Exodus itself: “from the day their ancestors came out of Egypt until this day.” The rebellion is not a recent development but a lifelong pattern — the Deuteronomistic History’s most comprehensive statement of Israel’s chronic covenant failure. The phrase “provoking my anger” (makhʿîsîm ʾōtî) — causing YHWH grief/anger — is the emotional register of divine relationship: the abandoned covenant partner.
21.16: “Moreover, Manasseh shed so much innocent blood (dām nāqî) that he filled Jerusalem from end to end.” Dām nāqî — “innocent blood” — is the specific term for judicial murder and extrajudicial killing (Gen 9:6; Deut 19:10; Jer 7:6; 22:3). The Targum and rabbinic tradition identify Manasseh’s killing of prophets, specifically Isaiah, as among these acts. The “from end to end” (peh ʾel peh — literally “mouth to mouth”) is a measure of completeness: the innocent blood saturated the city. The shedding of innocent blood is identified in the Deuteronomistic History as the sin that ultimately could not be atoned (24:4).
21.17: The regnal summary formula: acts and sins recorded in the annals. The notation “including the sin he committed” (wəḥaṭṭāʾtô ʾăšer ḥāṭāʾ) — “his sin which he sinned” — is an unusually redundant phrasing that the narrator uses to underscore that the annals contain incriminating material, not merely administrative record.
21.18: Manasseh is buried in the garden of his palace — gān ʿuzzāʾ, the Garden of Uzza — not in the City of David where the covenant kings were buried. The location of burial outside the royal tombs is theologically significant in Kings: the kings buried in the city of David are implicitly within the covenant succession; those buried elsewhere are distanced from it. Amon will be buried in the same garden (v26).
21.19: Amon: twenty-two years old, reigned two years. The brevity of his reign — two years — stands in stark contrast to his father’s fifty-five. The short reign is both historical fact and literary comment: Manasseh’s dynasty could not sustain itself. The mother’s name, Meshullemeth daughter of Haruz of Jotbah, is preserved — the Deuteronomistic History maintains the maternal genealogy for all Judean kings.
21.20: “He did what was evil in the sight of YHWH, as his father Manasseh had done.” The evaluative formula for Amon is the simplest possible: he repeated his father’s pattern without variation. The interesting contrast with Manasseh is that Amon receives no extended prophetic oracle and no attention to specific sins — his reign is too brief to establish independent theological significance. He is the continuation of Manasseh’s trajectory without Manasseh’s length.
21.21: “He walked in all the way his father walked, worshipping the idols (gillûlîm) his father had worshipped and bowing down to them.” Hālak bəderek — “to walk in the way of” — is the Deuteronomistic History’s standard moral evaluation formula. The negative form (“did not walk in the way of David”) is the condemnation; the positive form (“walked in all the way of his father”) — when the father is Manasseh — is equally damning. The gillûlîm recurs from v11: Amon inherits both the idols and the contempt they deserve.
21.22: “He abandoned (ʿāzaḇ) YHWH, the God of his ancestors, and did not walk in the way of YHWH.” ʿāzaḇ — “to abandon/forsake” — is the covenantal term for covenant desertion (Deut 28:20; 31:16; Jer 1:16). The contrast with Josiah’s mother’s name in 22:1 (Yᵉḏîḏāh — “beloved of YHWH”) is the narrator’s subtle marker of what is coming: out of Amon’s house comes the beloved king.
21.23: Amon’s officials (ʿăḇādāyw — his servants/courtiers) conspire and assassinate him in his palace. The conspiracy is palace-internal — not a popular revolt but a court coup. The motive is not stated; later scholarship has proposed anti-Assyrian or pro-reform factions, but the text does not explain. The narrator is interested in the theological, not the political, mechanism.
21.24: “The people of the land (ʿam hāʾāreṣ) killed all who had conspired against King Amon and made his son Josiah king in his place.” The ʿam hāʾāreṣ — “people of the land” — is a recurring political actor in Kings: the free landowning citizenry who function as a constitutional check on palace coups (cf. 11:14, 18-20; 14:21; 16:15). They punish the assassins and install the legitimate heir. Their action here protects the Davidic succession that YHWH promised to preserve and ensures that Josiah, not a usurper, occupies the throne.
21.25: Standard negative regnal summary for Amon. The brevity of the notice matches the brevity of the reign.
21.26: Amon buried in the Garden of Uzza — the same location as Manasseh (v18). Father and son share the garden burial, both excluded from the City of David’s covenant tombs. The dynasty resumes with Josiah, the great-grandson of Manasseh, who will reverse everything his ancestor set up.

22.1: Josiah was eight years old at his accession — the second child-king after Joash (2 Kgs 11:21), who also acceded to the throne after an assassination. The eight-year-old king will grow into the most faithful king of the Davidic line. The regnal length — thirty-one years — is the middle length, neither the extreme of Manasseh’s fifty-five nor the brevity of Amon’s two. Josiah’s mother’s name, Yᵉḏîḏāh (“beloved of YHWH”), is programmatic: the beloved king’s mother carries the divine epithet.
22.2: “He did what was right in the sight of YHWH and followed completely the way of his ancestor David, not turning aside to the right or to the left.” The phrase “not turning aside right or left” (lōʾ sār yāmîn ûśmōʾl) is the Deuteronomic formula for perfect covenant fidelity (Deut 5:32; 17:20; Josh 1:7). It is applied only to David and Josiah in the entire Deuteronomistic History — the two kings who fully embody covenant faithfulness. The comparison is to “his ancestor David” (kᵉkol derek dāwîḏ ʾāḇîyw) — not merely “as David did” but “in the complete way of David.”
22.3: In Josiah’s eighteenth year he sends Shaphan to the temple for the repair. The eighteenth year (621 BCE) is the theological pivot of the narrative — the year of the book’s discovery. Shaphan (šāpān — rock hyrax) becomes one of the most important figures in the late monarchic period: he and his family (Ahikam, Gedaliah) repeatedly protect prophets and reformers (Jer 26:24; 36:10-12; 40:5-6). The sōpēr (secretary/scribe) was a senior state official with both administrative and literary responsibilities.
22.4: Hilkiah the high priest (hakkōhēn haggādôl) is to count the money brought to the temple — temple income from donors channeled through doorkeepers. The administrative machinery of Josiah’s reform is organized: money gathered, supervisors appointed, workers paid. The repair of the temple is the physical analog to the spiritual repair Josiah will undertake — both require systematic attention to what has been neglected.
22.5: The repair supervisors (pəqûḏê habbayit) are to pay the workers. The temple workers are ʿōśê hāmᵉlāʾkāh — “those who do the work” — the same phrase used for the craftsmen of the Mosaic tabernacle (Exod 36:2, 4, 8). The terminology links Josiah’s temple repair to the original construction of Israel’s sacred dwelling.
22.6: Carpenters (ḥārāšîm), builders (bōnîm), stonemasons (gōdᵉrê ḥômaʿ), timber (ʿēṣ), and dressed stone (ʾabnê māḥṣēḇ). The listing of specific building trades and materials reflects the administrative precision of the reform project. The Deuteronomistic historian includes the detail to establish that the temple repair was real and systematic, not a symbolic gesture.
22.7: “They were not required to keep accounts for the money entrusted to them, because they were acting honestly (ʾĕmet).” ʾĔmet — faithfulness/reliability/truth — is the term for covenantal integrity (Exod 18:21; Ps 15:2). The workers’ ʾĕmet is what makes accounting unnecessary: where there is faithfulness, there is no need for oversight. The same principle operated in the earlier temple repair of Joash (2 Kgs 12:15). The exemption from accounting is a literary marker of moral integrity.
22.8: Hilkiah announces to Shaphan: “I have found the sēfer hattôrāh — the Book of the Law — in the house of YHWH.” This is the most significant textual discovery in the OT narrative. The sēfer hattôrāh is widely identified in scholarship as some form of Deuteronomy, given that the subsequent reform precisely targets Deuteronomy’s prohibitions. The verb māṣāʾ — “found” — carries the covenantal resonance of “discovering what was lost.” Hilkiah gives the book to Shaphan; Shaphan reads it; then reads it to the king.
22.9: Shaphan’s report to the king covers two items: (1) the money has been disbursed to the supervisors and workers, and (2) Hilkiah has given him a book. The administrative item first, then the potentially explosive textual item — the sequence mirrors the administrative context in which the discovery was embedded. Temple repair finances led to the Torah’s rediscovery: the material act of restoration and the textual act are linked.
22.10: Shaphan reads the book aloud to the king — wayyiqrāʾhû ʿal pᵉnê hammelek, “read it before the face of the king.” The oral reading is the primary mode of Torah reception (Deut 31:11-13; Neh 8:1-8): the king must hear the law, not merely possess it. The Torah is not a private text but a public, oral covenant document. The reading before the king anticipates the covenant-reading before all Israel in ch23.
22.11: “When the king heard the words of the Book of the Law, he tore his clothes (wayyiqraʿ ʾet bᵉgādāyw).” The tearing of garments is the physical expression of grief and crisis: Jacob at Joseph’s supposed death (Gen 37:34), David at Saul’s death (2 Sam 1:11), Hezekiah at Sennacherib’s ultimatum (19:1). For Josiah, the grief is specifically covenantal: the hearing of the Torah’s curses in light of Israel’s manifest non-compliance produces the grief of a king who understands what the book means for his people.
22.12: Josiah dispatches a five-person delegation to “inquire of YHWH” (dərəšû ʾet YHWH) — the technical term for prophetic consultation (1 Kgs 22:5, 8; Jer 21:2). The delegation includes the high priest, a court official, the secretary, and the king’s attendant — senior representatives of temple, court, and administration. The fact that Josiah sends others rather than going himself is not evasion but protocol: the king sends delegations for oracular consultation.
22.13: “Inquire of YHWH for me, for the people, and for all Judah about what is written in this book that has been found. Great is YHWH’s anger that burns against us because our ancestors did not obey (šāmᵉʿû) the words of this book, to do according to everything written for us.” Josiah’s framing of the inquiry is remarkable: he immediately reads the book as a word of judgment already in process, not as a future threat. The “for us” (ʿālênû) shows that he identifies with his ancestors’ failure, not as a spectator. The šmʿ-failure (not obeying/listening) is the exact reverse of the šmʿ-command that Deuteronomy begins with: “Hear (šᵉmaʿ), O Israel.”
22.14: The delegation goes to Huldah the prophetess — hûlḏāh hannᵉḇîʾāh — wife of Shallum, keeper of the wardrobe, living in the Second District of Jerusalem. The choice of Huldah over the contemporary prophets Jeremiah (active from 627 BCE) and Zephaniah (active under Josiah) has generated scholarly discussion; the narrative offers no explanation. Huldah is one of four named female prophets in the OT (Miriam, Deborah, Huldah, Noadiah — Noadiah is a false prophet). Her oracle authenticates the book and determines Judah’s response to it. The “keeper of the wardrobe” (šōmēr habbᵉgādîm) — her husband’s title — indicates an inner-court official position.
22.15: “This is what YHWH, the God of Israel, says: Tell the man who sent you to me” — the standard prophetic messenger formula (kōh ʾāmar YHWH), indicating that Huldah speaks as YHWH’s authorized messenger. She does not speak in her own name but transmits the divine word. The referential “the man who sent you” (not “the king”) may reflect the formal distance of prophetic address or a deliberate de-emphasizing of royal status.
22.16: “I am going to bring disaster on this place and its people, according to everything written in the book the king of Judah has read.” Huldah confirms two things: (1) the book is authentic — it is YHWH’s word; (2) the judgment it announces is certain and will come. The “book the king of Judah has read” is the standard by which history is being measured, and the measurement finds the people wanting. Josiah’s reform, no matter how thorough, will not reverse the verdict — it will only determine the timing.
22.17: “Because they have abandoned me (ʿāzᵉḇûnî) and burned incense to other gods, provoking my anger (lᵉmaʿan hakhʿîsēnî) through everything their hands have done, my anger (ḥărātî) will burn against this place and will not be quenched (lōʾ tiḵbeh).” The ʿāzaḇ-ḥārāh-lōʾ tiḵbeh sequence — abandonment leading to anger that cannot be extinguished — is the covenant-curse vocabulary of Deuteronomy (Deut 29:20). The fire of divine anger echoes Elijah’s fire at Carmel, but this is the fire of judgment, not vindication.
22.18: The oracle pivots to address Josiah specifically: “As for the king of Judah who sent you to inquire of YHWH — say this to him.” The personal oracle for Josiah is separated from the national oracle by the structural marker “say this to him.” The distinction is crucial: collective judgment and individual exception occupy the same prophetic word simultaneously.
22.19: “Because your heart was responsive (wayyikkānēʿ lᵉḇāḇᵉkā) and you humbled yourself before YHWH when you heard what I spoke against this place and its people.” Kānāʿ — to humble/subdue — is used only twice in the Deuteronomistic History in this self-humbling sense: 1 Kgs 21:29 of Ahab, and here of Josiah. Both humiliations temporarily defer divine judgment. The term carries the sense of a high thing brought low — the king who lowers himself before the word earns the word’s mercy. YHWH specifies what he heard: (1) tearing garments; (2) weeping before him. The physical acts of grief are the evidence of the heart’s response.
22.20: “Therefore I will gather you to your ancestors, and you will be buried in peace (bəšālôm). Your eyes will not see all the disaster I am going to bring on this place.” The promise of a peaceful death is the personal reward for Josiah’s humility. The word šālôm — wholeness/peace — is the opposite of the curse. Josiah will be gathered to his ancestors before the catastrophe. The irony that follows in 23:29-30 is sharp: Josiah dies in battle at Megiddo, which seems inconsistent with “buried in peace.” The rabbinic and Christian interpretation is that “in peace” means before the exile — he does not live to see Jerusalem fall, which is the peace promised.

23.1: Josiah “summoned all the elders of Judah and Jerusalem” — the political assembly precedes the covenantal assembly. The gathering of the elders (ziqnê) as covenant witnesses is the Mosaic pattern: Moses summoned the elders for the Passover institution (Exod 12:21) and covenant ratification (Exod 24:1). The covenantal event requires representative witnesses.
23.2: The king goes to the house of YHWH with all the men of Judah, all the inhabitants of Jerusalem, the priests, the prophets, and all the people from the least to the greatest — lᵉmiqāṭōn wəʿad gādôl. The universality formula marks this as a covenant assembly, not merely a royal ceremony. The king reads “in their ears all the words of the Book of the Covenant” (sēfer habbᵉrît) — the public oral reading of the covenant text, the renewal of the Sinai scene. The reading is loud enough to enter their ears: covenant hearing precedes covenant commitment.
23.3: “The king stood beside the pillar (ʿāmad ʿal hāʿammûḏ) and renewed the covenant before YHWH.” The pillar/column was the royal standing place in the temple (cf. 11:14; 2 Chr 34:31). Josiah’s renewed covenant includes three Deuteronomic elements: (1) lālek ʾaḥar YHWH — to follow/walk after YHWH; (2) lišmōr miṣwōtāyw — to keep his commandments; (3) bəkol lēḇ ûḇəkol nepeš — with all heart and all soul. The last phrase is the beginning of the Shema (Deut 6:5) applied covenantally. “All the people entered the covenant” (wayyaʿămōḏ kol hāʿām babbᵉrît) — the covenant is not imposed but collectively ratified.
23.4: The systematic purge begins: all vessels made for Baal, Asherah, and the starry hosts are removed from the temple and burned at the Kidron Valley — the valley east of Jerusalem that served as the city’s ritual dump site (cf. 1 Kgs 15:13; 2 Kgs 23:6, 12). The ash is then carried to Bethel — sending the ash of Judah’s idols to the epicenter of Israel’s apostasy. The ʾidolatrous priests (kᵉmārîm) — a word used only for illegitimate cult personnel — are named for dismissal.
23.5: The kᵉmārîm — a contemptuous term for pagan priests — who burned incense (wayyaqqēṭer) at the high places in Judah’s towns are dismissed. The targets include the cults of the sun, moon, constellations (mazzālôt), and all the starry hosts — the full Mesopotamian astral pantheon Manasseh had introduced. Bāmôt (high places) from Jerusalem’s surroundings to Geba in the north and Beersheba in the south are defiled.
23.6: The Asherah pole is removed from the house of YHWH — the object Manasseh had placed inside the temple (21:7) — taken to the Kidron Valley, burned, ground to dust, and the dust scattered over the graves of common people. The five-step destruction sequence (remove, burn, grind to dust, scatter on graves) is the most thorough possible desecration of the cult object: the graves of the common people, being ritually defiling, contaminate whatever is scattered on them.
23.7: The quarters of the qᵉḏēšîm (weˀet-bāttê haqqᵉḏēšîm) are torn down — the male shrine prostitutes (qādēš, masculine of qᵉḏēšāh) whose quarters were inside the temple complex, where women wove hangings (bāttîm) for the Asherah. The combination of male cult prostitution and the Asherah weaving lodge in the temple precinct indicates how thoroughly the Solomonic temple had been converted to syncretistic use during Manasseh’s reign.
23.8: Josiah brings all the priests from the cities of Judah to Jerusalem and defiles (wayᵉṭammēʾ) the high places from Geba to Beersheba. The defilement is ritual: by bringing human bones or other impurity into contact with the high places, they are rendered unusable for worship. The centralization of the priesthood in Jerusalem follows Deut 12:5-7: all sacrifice is to happen at the single chosen place, not at local high places.
23.9: The priests from the defiled high places are not permitted to serve at the Jerusalem altar — there is a hierarchy of cultic purity even within the reform. They may eat unleavened bread among their priestly brothers but not serve at the altar. The matzot (unleavened bread) here likely refers to the priestly portion of offerings; the high-place priests are maintained but their altar service is permanently restricted. This nuanced approach — not executing them but restricting them — is a pastoral accommodation of existing cultic personnel.
23.10: “He desecrated (waytammēʾ) Topheth, which is in the Valley of Ben-Hinnom (gê ben hinnōm), so that no one could sacrifice his son or daughter in the fire to Molech.” Tōpet — the fire-altar site in the valley south of Jerusalem — was the location of child sacrifice to Molech, practiced by Manasseh (21:6) and apparently by many in Jerusalem (Jer 7:31-32; 19:5-6). The gê ben hinnōm (Valley of Ben-Hinnom) will contract in later Hebrew to gêhinnōm — “Gehenna” — and become the NT’s primary term for the place of eschatological fire (Matt 5:22, 29-30). Josiah’s defilement of the site is the historical action behind the name; the valley that was the site of infant burning becomes the image of divine judgment.
23.11: The horses dedicated to the sun (hassûsîm ʾăšer nātᵉnû malkê yᵉhûḏāh lāšemeš) — sun-worship in ANE religion involved ritual horse processions dedicated to the sun deity — are removed from the temple entrance and their solar chariots are burned. The astral cults involved elaborate cultic paraphernalia; the Assyrian sun-worship that Manasseh promoted included horse and chariot dedications documented in both biblical and extra-biblical texts.
23.12: Josiah pulls down the roof altars built by Ahaz (the ʿăliyyōt ʾāḥāz) and the altars built by Manasseh in the two courts of the temple. The accumulated idolatry of two previous kings is systematically demolished. The ashes are cast into the Kidron — the ritual dumping ground. The temple is being stripped back to its original Solomonic configuration.
23.13: “The king desecrated the high places that were east of Jerusalem on the south side of the Mount of Corruption (har hammašḥît) — the shrines Solomon king of Israel had built for Ashtoreth the detestable god of the Sidonians, for Chemosh the detestable god of Moab, and for Milcom the detestable god of the Ammonites.” The “Mount of Corruption” is the south ridge of the Mount of Olives — called har hammišḥāh (“mount of anointing”) in 11:7, now har hammašḥît (“mount of corruption/destruction”) — a wordplay that marks Solomon’s idolatrous high places as the corruption of what should have been a place of anointing. These shrines had stood for over 300 years since Solomon built them (1 Kgs 11:7-8).
23.14: The sacred stones (maṣṣēḇôt) are smashed — the standing stones that functioned as Canaanite cultic markers, prohibited by Deut 12:3 and Exod 23:24. The Asherah poles (ʾăšērîm) are cut down. The sites are covered with human bones (ʿaṣāmôt) — the most powerful available defiling agent (Num 19:16). The systematic use of human bones to defile cultic sites is a form of permanent disqualification: a site covered with bones can never again be used for sacrifice.
23.15: Even the Bethel altar — the oldest and most symbolically powerful site of the northern apostasy, built by Jeroboam son of Nebat (1 Kgs 12:28-29) — is demolished, burned, ground to dust, and the Asherah burned. The prophecy of the unnamed man of God from Judah (1 Kgs 13:2) — who foretold that “a son named Josiah will be born to the house of David and will burn human bones on you” — is here fulfilled, approximately 300 years after it was spoken. The fulfillment demonstrates that YHWH’s prophetic word is not merely predictive but historically executive.
23.16: Josiah notices the tombs on the hillside and has bones taken from them and burned on the Bethel altar — defiling it with human bones exactly as the man of God had prophesied (“bones burned on it,” 1 Kgs 13:2). The 300-year gap between prophecy and fulfillment is the narrator’s implicit argument for the reliability of the prophetic word: no human planner could have staged this.
23.17: Josiah inquires about a particular monument and is told it is the tomb of “the man of God who came from Judah and predicted these things which you have done against the altar at Bethel.” The irony is layered: the man of God whose bones were buried at Bethel was the prophet whose disobedience on the return journey led to his death (1 Kgs 13:11-32), yet his prophecy was entirely accurate. The accuracy of the prophecy transcended the prophet’s personal failure — the word YHWH gave him was true regardless of his subsequent disobedience.
23.18: “Leave him alone; let no one disturb his bones.” Josiah preserves both the man of God’s tomb and the tomb of the Samarian prophet who buried him (1 Kgs 13:31) — the two are protected together. The preservation honors the prophetic word that has just been fulfilled; the man of God’s bones are the prophetic marker of what has happened.
23.19: Josiah extends the reform to Samaria — the former northern kingdom now under Assyrian provincial administration — removing the shrines at the high places and doing “everything he had done at Bethel.” The reach of Josiah’s reform into the former northern territory is possible because Assyrian power is waning (Assyria falls to Babylon in 612 BCE); Josiah fills the political vacuum with the reform. The inclusion of Samarian high places represents the implicit reunification of Israel and Judah under the covenant standard.
23.20: Josiah slaughters all the priests of the northern high places on their own altars and burns human bones on them. The parallel to the 1 Kgs 13:2 prophecy is now fully enacted. The slaughter of the high-place priests in Samaria is more severe than the treatment of Judean high-place priests (v9: those were restricted but not killed) — the northern apostasy, being longer and more entrenched, receives harsher treatment.
23.21: “The king commanded all the people: Celebrate the Passover to YHWH your God, as it is written in this Book of the Covenant.” The Passover command closes the reform cycle: the book was found (ch22), the covenant was renewed (23:1-3), the cultic apparatus of apostasy was dismantled (23:4-20), and now the foundational festival is restored. Passover is the feast that defines Israel as the redeemed people of YHWH (Exod 12:14); its restoration after a long lapse is the resumption of the foundational covenantal identity.
23.22: “Not since the days of the judges who led Israel, nor throughout the entire period of the kings of Israel and the kings of Judah, had any such Passover been celebrated.” The scope of the comparison is extraordinary: from the Judges through the entire united and divided monarchy, no Passover of this magnitude had been kept. The superlative marks Josiah’s Passover as the culmination of the covenant renewal — the most complete single act of covenant restoration in Israel’s history.
23.23: The specification: “in the eighteenth year of King Josiah.” The eighteenth year is the year of the book’s discovery (22:3) — the covenant renewal and the Passover restoration are accomplished in the same year. The eighteenth year is the theological center of the entire Josiah narrative: it is the year in which YHWH’s Torah is recovered and Israel’s foundational festival is restored.
23.24: Josiah removes the mediums, spiritists, household gods (ṭərāpîm), idols (gillûlîm), and all detestable things seen in Judah and Jerusalem — completing the list from Manasseh’s reign (21:6). The ṭərāpîm (household gods) are among the oldest and most persistent forms of Israelite syncretism (Gen 31:19-35; Judg 17:5; 1 Sam 19:13; Ezek 21:21). The comprehensiveness of the list signals that Josiah has reversed everything Manasseh instituted, matching item for item.
23.25: “Neither before nor after Josiah was there a king like him who turned to YHWH with all his heart (bəkol lᵉḇāḇô), with all his soul (bəkol napšô), and with all his might (ûḇəkol mᵉʾōḏô), in accordance with all the Law of Moses.” The three-part phrase is the Shema (Deut 6:5) applied to covenantal faithfulness. The superlative is total: Josiah is the king who most fully embodied the Shema. The comparison includes “before” — Josiah exceeds even David. Yet the following verse begins with the devastating ʾak — “nevertheless.”
23.26: “Nevertheless (ʾak), YHWH did not turn away from the burning of his great anger, which blazed against Judah because of all that Manasseh had done to provoke him.” The ʾak is the most devastating single word in the Josiah narrative: after the superlative praise of v25, it introduces the irreversibility of the divine judgment. Manasseh’s sin was not merely great but structurally final — it set in motion a judgment that even the best subsequent king could not reverse. The innocent blood of v21:16 is specifically named in 24:4 as the sin that YHWH “was not willing to pardon.”
23.27: “So YHWH said, ‘I will remove Judah from my sight as I removed Israel. I will reject Jerusalem, the city I chose, and this temple, about which I said, “There shall my Name be.”’” The divine speech cancels the election promise explicitly: the city chosen, the temple named — both will be rejected. The māʾas (reject) applied to Jerusalem echoes the rejection of Saul (1 Sam 15:23, 26; 16:1) and Israel (2 Kgs 17:20). YHWH’s māʾas is always the consequence of the covenant partner’s prior abandonment of the relationship.
23.28: Josiah’s regnal summary and reference to the annals. The standard closing formula marks the end of the Josiah account. The disaster of Megiddo (v29-30) falls outside the formal regnal summary, indicating it is epilogic rather than central to the narrator’s theological evaluation.
23.29: Pharaoh Necho of Egypt marches to the Euphrates to support Assyria against the rising Babylonian empire. Josiah goes to intercept him at Megiddo — why is unstated. The possibilities: pro-Babylonian strategy, territorial defense, or misguided messianic zeal. Necho kills him at Megiddo. The terseness of the Hebrew — wayyᵉmîtēhû ḇᵉmegiddô kᵉrᵉʾōtô, “he killed him at Megiddo when he encountered him” — is stark: the greatest covenant king dies in three words. Megiddo (later Harmāgᵉdôn in Rev 16:16) is the place where Canaan’s forces were defeated by Deborah and Barak (Judg 5:19) and where Ahaziah died (9:27); it accumulates military-eschatological significance.
23.30: Josiah’s body is brought from Megiddo to Jerusalem by his servants in a chariot and buried in his own tomb. The ʿam hāʾāreṣ — the people of the land, who had made him king after Amon’s assassination — anoint his son Jehoahaz. The same constitutional actors who preserved the Davidic line after Amon’s coup (21:24) now install the new king after Josiah’s unexpected death. The burial in Jerusalem, in his own tomb, fulfills Huldah’s oracle: “you will be gathered to your ancestors” (22:20) — he dies before the exile.
23.31: Jehoahaz, twenty-three years old, reigns three months. The brevity — the same as Amon’s — signals the rapid unraveling of everything Josiah built. His mother Hamutal, daughter of Jeremiah of Libnah, is noted — a different Jeremiah from the prophet. The maternal genealogy is maintained even in the post-Josiah collapse.
23.32: “He did what was evil in the sight of YHWH, just as his predecessors had done.” The formula reverts to the pre-Josiah standard, applied after a single verse of introduction. Josiah’s reform has not taken root in his sons. The covenantal inheritance depends on the heart, not the institution; Josiah’s personal Shema-faithfulness did not transmit to his heirs.
23.33: Pharaoh Necho imprisons Jehoahaz at Riblah in Hamath and imposes a tribute of one hundred talents of silver and a talent of gold on the land. Riblah in Hamath will become the place of judgment for the last Judean kings (25:6, 20-21). Egypt asserts tributary control over Judah; the political independence of the Davidic monarchy is functionally over.
23.34: Necho installs Eliakim son of Josiah as king and renames him Jehoiakim. The name-change (ʿāzaḇ... wayyāśem) from ʾElyāqîm (“God establishes”) to Yəhôyāqîm (“YHWH establishes”) is a marker of Egyptian authority over the divine naming of Judah’s king: Necho substitutes the divine name in the king’s name as an act of ownership. The exiling of Jehoahaz to Egypt, where he dies (Jer 22:11-12), removes the ʿam hāʾāreṣ’s chosen king permanently.
23.35: Jehoiakim taxes the land to pay Necho’s tribute — requiring each person to contribute according to their assessed wealth (kᵉʿerkô). The tribute system (100 talents silver, 1 talent gold) is extracted from the population rather than from the royal treasury, shifting the burden of imperial vassalage to the people. The same administrative extraction that Solomon used (1 Kgs 4:7) returns under Egyptian compulsion.
23.36: Jehoiakim’s regnal notice: twenty-five years old, eleven-year reign. The eleven years spans the transition from Egyptian to Babylonian dominance (605 BCE: Battle of Carchemish). His mother Zebidah daughter of Pedaiah of Rumah is noted — the maternal genealogy continues.
23.37: “He did what was evil in the sight of YHWH, just as his predecessors had done.” The final verse of chapter 23 closes the chapter on the same formula that opened Jehoahaz’s brief reign. The post-Josiah collapse is complete: the two sons who follow him both receive the standard condemnation. The last word of chapter 23 in Hebrew is ʾăḇōtāyw — “his fathers” — the kings who preceded him. Josiah’s reform has left no successor; the “fathers” have triumphed over the son.

4.1: nᵉšî ʾîš — "the wife of a man (of)": the phrase identifies her entirely by her relation to her dead husband, a member of the prophetic community (bᵉnê hannᵉḇiʾîm). Her direct appeal — "you know that your servant feared (yārēʾ) the LORD" — invokes the covenant connection between prophetic piety and prophetic responsibility. The creditor (nōšeh, from H5383 — to lend on interest, with the social weight of Lev 25:39-41) can legally enslave children for unpaid debt; the social background is attested in Neh 5:5. The widow has a legal problem only a prophet can solve.
4.2: ʾăsûḵ šemen — "a jar of oil." The word ʾāsûḵ (H610) is rare — a small vessel for anointing oil, insufficient for cooking or commerce. The MKT's "jar" captures the sense; but the Hebrew emphasizes smallness and singularity: she has exactly nothing useful except this one anointing flask. The miracle proceeds not from abundance but from the scarcest possible resource — a pattern recurrent in the Elisha cycle (ch. 4:42-44; 2 Kgs 3:17) and deliberate: the prophet multiplies what the petitioner offers, not what he carries.
4.3: kēlîm rêqîm — "empty vessels." The adjective rêq (H7386 — empty, hollow, vain) is the same word used in Gen 37:24 for the empty pit into which Joseph was thrown. The instruction to gather many empty vessels before any miracle has begun is a faith requirement: the quantity of vessels gathered limits the quantity of oil received (v6 confirms this). The negative command "do not get too few" (ʾal tamʿîṭû) suggests the disciples' tendency to underestimate.
4.4: sāgᵉrî haddālet baʿăḏēḵ — "shut the door behind you." The instruction is repeated in v5 as the action is performed. Elisha himself is absent from the miracle; the widow and her sons pour oil in private, behind a closed door. The prophetic miracle requires neither the prophet's presence nor a public audience — the sāgar (shut/close) emphasizes enclosure and exclusivity. This private modality contrasts with Elijah's confrontational, public miracles and anticipates Jesus's instruction to pray in the inner room with the door shut (Matt 6:6).
4.5: wayyagsîšû ʾēlehā... wᵉhîʾ mōṣāqet — "they kept bringing her vessels... and she kept pouring." The verb yāṣaq (H3332, pour out) is an imperfect/continuous form here; the Hebrew syntax places her continuous pouring against their continuous bringing. The miracle operates dynamically in the act of pouring — not in the original jar — and ceases only when the vessels run out. The oil flows not from a store but from a divine provision activated by the act of faith.
4.6: wayyaʿᵃmod haššāmen — "the oil stopped." The verb ʿāmaḏ (H5975, stand/stop) is the same used for the Jordan waters "standing" at the crossing (Josh 3:16). When the last vessel fills, the oil stops — not before, not after. The miracle is precisely calibrated to available capacity: the widow's faith in gathering vessels determines the scope of the miracle. The theological implication is that the limit was not in the oil (divine supply) but in the containers gathered (human preparation).
4.7: šallᵉmî ʾet nᵉšēḵî — "pay your debt." The verb šālem (H7999 — pay, complete, make šālôm) connects the economic resolution to the broader šālôm theme of the passage. Elisha's instruction is practical and complete: sell the oil, pay the debt (nāšeḵî — literally "your bite/interest," from the same root as the creditor's title), and live on the surplus. The miracle produces enough to resolve the crisis and sustain the family — no surplus beyond need, but precisely sufficient.
4.8: ʾiššāh gᵉḏôlāh — "a great/wealthy woman." The phrase uses gāḏôl (H1419 — great) without specifying in what respect; in context it suggests both social standing and wealth. The verb ḥāzaq (H2388 — seize, urge strongly) describes her compelling Elisha to eat: she does not merely invite but presses (wayyiḥzaq ʿālāyw). The regularity is established: "whenever he passed (kol-pāʿam, every time he passed), he turned in there to eat." The initiative is entirely hers.
4.9: ʾîš ʾĕlōhîm qāḏôš — "a holy man of God." The title qāḏôš (H6918 — holy, set apart) is remarkable: this is the only occurrence in the Kings narrative where a human figure is called "holy" outright. The Shunammite's perception ("I perceive," yāḏaʿtî, perfect — I know with certainty) is theological discernment, not social observation. The verb ʿābar (pass through, H5674) in the participle ʿōḇēr + tāmîḏ (continually, H8548) establishes the regularity of the prophetic presence she has recognized and serves.
4.10: The four items — bed (miṭṭāh), table (šulḥān), chair (kissēʾ), and lamp (mᵉnôrāh) — constitute the minimal furniture for a functional room: sleep, eating, seating, and light. The lamp (mᵉnôrāh) is the same word used for the tabernacle lampstand (Exod 25:31ff); its inclusion in the list may not be incidental. The room is called a ʿăliyyāh (H5944 — upper room, roof chamber) — the word for the upper room in which Christ holds his final Passover (Mark 14:15; Luke 22:12) and where the disciples gather at Pentecost (Acts 1:13).
4.11: wayyānāḥ šāmmāh — "he rested there." The verb nûaḥ (H5117 — rest, settle) describes Elisha's use of the room prepared for him. The Shunammite's gift of restful hospitality — a private space for the prophet — is rewarded with the miraculous provision of a son. The structural parallel to Abraham and Sarah (three visitors, a son promised — Gen 18) is made explicit in v16. Rest precedes revelation.
4.12: Elisha communicates with the Shunammite through his servant Gehazi — a distance mediated by hierarchy. The use of šālaḥ (send) and the summons through Gehazi suggests that Elisha observes prophetic decorum with respect to an unrelated married woman. When the Shunammite "stands before him" (waqqāmet lᵉpānāyw), the phrase echoes royal/court language: standing before a superior.
4.13: ḥāradtî ʾēlênû — "you have gone to all this trouble for us." The verb ḥāraḏ (H2729 — tremble, be anxious, exert oneself) in the Hiphil indicates the care and effort she has taken. Her response — "I live among my own people" (bᵉtôḵ ʿammî ʾānōḵî yōšābet) — uses tāwek (midst/among) with ʿam (people/kinship group): she is embedded in her social network and does not need royal patronage. The answer is a gracious but clear statement of self-sufficiency.
4.14: Gehazi's observation — "she has no son and her husband is old" (bēn ʾên lāh wᵉʾîšāh zāqēn) — uses zāqēn (H2205 — old, elder) for the aged husband, the same word used for Abraham's advanced age in the son-of-promise narratives (Gen 18:11; 24:1). The barrenness-and-aged-husband combination places this narrative deliberately in the succession of promised-birth stories: Sarah, Rebekah, Rachel, Hannah. The verbal echoes signal that what follows will be another case of divine provision against biological impossibility.
4.15: She stands in the doorway (bappetaḥ — at the opening/threshold) rather than entering: the threshold marks a boundary of appropriate social access. Her position at the threshold mirrors the social negotiation of the entire episode — hospitality and honor-distance operating simultaneously.
4.16: ḥōḇeqet bēn — "embracing a son." The verb ḥāḇaq (H2263 — to embrace, clasp) is the specific word for the physical embrace of a child; it appears in Song 2:6; 8:3 and Gen 33:4 for familial embrace. The announcement — "at this season (kāʿēt ḥayyāh)" — uses the same phrase as Gen 18:10 when YHWH announces Isaac's birth to Abraham and Sarah ("at this season of life/next year"). ḥayyāh (H2416) may mean "next year" or "in the season of living/spring." The verbal echo to the Genesis annunciation is exact and deliberate.
4.17: The fulfillment — "she conceived and bore a son at that season the following year" — uses kāʿēt ḥayyāh again (fulfilling the exact phrase of the announcement, v16) and the verb yālaḏ (bear, H3205). The kāʾăšer dibbēr ("just as [Elisha] had spoken") uses the standard formula for prophetic fulfillment, confirming that the announcement was a prophetic word, not merely a social prediction.
4.18: The transition wayyigdal hayyeleḏ — "the child grew" — is the standard growth formula of OT narrative (Gen 21:8; 1 Sam 2:21); it moves the narrative forward to the crisis. ḥōṣᵉrîm (H2784 — reapers) identifies the season (harvest) and the social setting (agricultural labor) of the child's illness.
4.19: rōʾšî rōʾšî — "my head, my head!" The doubled form expresses intensity and urgency; the repetition is a child's distress call. The description is consistent with heatstroke (harvest, midday heat). The father's immediate delegation to the mother — not an attempt at treatment himself — shows the mother's centrality to this narrative.
4.20: wayyāmot baṣṣohŏrāyim — "he died at noon." The death is stated simply, without euphemism, with a specific time. Noon (ṣohŏrāyim) reinforces the heat-narrative. The directness of wayyāmot (H4191, and he died) after the doubled cry of distress creates a stark, unambiguous statement of the crisis that the rest of the chapter resolves.
4.21: wayyaššiḵēhû ʿal miṭṭat ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm — "she laid him on the bed of the man of God." This is the Shunammite's first act, before summoning a donkey or telling her husband. Placing the dead child on the prophet's bed is an act of faith that the prophet's connection to YHWH extends even to death. The shut door (wayyisgor baddᵉlet baʿăḏô) seals the dead child in the prophetic space — echoing the shut door of the oil miracle (v4-5), where enclosure preceded supernatural provision.
4.22: The Shunammite requests a donkey and a servant to go to the man of God — without explaining why. Her husband's question ("why go today?") treats the journey as irregular: it is not a festival day (rōʾš ḥōḏeš, new moon) or Sabbath, the usual days for visiting a prophet. Her answer — šālôm — is the first of three instances in this passage where she uses the peace-formula while facing disaster.
4.23: šālôm (first use in this passage). The word carries the full weight of covenantal "all is well" — wholeness, completion, soundness. The Shunammite says šālôm when the situation is anything but well: her son is dead. This is not deception but a theological statement — she trusts YHWH's governance and refuses to frame the situation as final before the prophet has responded. The grammar supports this: she does not say "it was šālôm"; she says "šālôm" as a present-tense declaration.
4.24: ʿeṣôr wᵉlōʾ taʿᵃṣar lî lirkōb — "drive on and do not slow down for me to ride." The causative phrase is urgent: the Shunammite commands haste and forbids any deceleration on her account. ʿāṣar (H6113 — restrain, hold back) appears twice in two clauses. The urgency of her journey is expressed through repeated negatives of restraint: do not slow down unless I tell you.
4.25: hinnēh haššûnammît hallezyāh — "there is the Shunammite woman!" Elisha's perception of her approach from a distance (mērāḥôq) before her arrival establishes his awareness of the situation. The exclamatory hinnēh (behold) signals dramatic recognition. He sends Gehazi to meet her — but the Shunammite will bypass the intermediary to reach the prophet himself (v27).
4.26: Gehazi's three-question greeting — "is it well (šālôm) with you? well with your husband? well with your child?" — probes the situation systematically. The Shunammite answers the entire triple question with one word: šālôm. This is the third šālôm in the passage (see vv22-23). The Hebrew reads wayyōʾmer šālôm — "and she said: peace." She has not abandoned grief (v27 shows her mar nāpeš — bitter of soul), but she refuses to articulate the crisis to Gehazi; she will speak only to the prophet who has the authority to act.
4.27: mar nāpeš — "bitter of soul/spirit." Mar (H4751 — bitter) + nāpeš (H5315 — self/soul/breath/life) is the Hebrew idiom for intense anguish; the same phrase describes Hannah's grief (1 Sam 1:10) and Job's (Job 3:20; 7:11). The admission that follows — "YHWH has hidden it from me and has not told me" (YHWH hisᵉtîr mimmennî wᵉlōʾ higgîḏ lî) — is a candid acknowledgment of prophetic limitation: the man of God does not have omniscient access to all divine purposes. H6845 (sātar, conceal) in the Hiphil: YHWH deliberately concealed this from Elisha.
4.28: hăšāʾaltî bēn mēʾēt ʾăḏōnî — "Did I ask a son from my lord?" The verb šāʾal (H7592 — ask, request, demand) makes the accusation sharp: she did not request a son; Elisha volunteered the gift. Her second question — "Did I not say, Do not deceive me?" — uses tašlēʾ from H7952 (to mislead, throw astray). The MKT's "give false hope" captures the semantic range: she had warned him at the announcement not to raise hopes that would not materialize. Now she holds him to that responsibility.
4.29: ḥᵃgōr mᵉtnêḵā — "gird your loins." The idiom (H2296 + H4975) means to tuck one's robe into the belt for rapid movement — standard preparation for urgent travel or action (1 Kgs 18:46; 2 Kgs 9:1). The staff (mišʿantî, H4938) is the prophet's distinctive implement; in Elisha's instruction it functions as an extension of prophetic authority, delegated to the servant. The command not to greet anyone on the road (ʾim yiṣṣālᵉḵā ʾîš lōʾ tᵉḇārᵉḵennû) is a formula for maximum haste — social greetings in the ancient Near East were time-consuming rituals.
4.30: ḥay YHWH wᵉḥay napšeḵā — "As YHWH lives and as your soul lives." The double oath formula (H2416 × 2) with YHWH and the prophet's own life invoked is the strongest available oath formula in biblical Hebrew. The Shunammite binds herself to Elisha personally: she will not leave without him. Her insistence overrides the delegated authority of the staff — she knows, at some level, that the prophet's personal presence is required for what she needs. Elisha follows her, as Elijah followed the widow of Zarephath in an analogous crisis (1 Kgs 17).
4.31: wᵉlōʾ qôl wᵉlōʾ qešeḇ — "no voice and no attention." Qôl (H6963 — voice, sound) and qešeḇ (H7182 — attentiveness, response) together describe complete non-response. The staff without the prophet produces nothing: Gehazi's report — "the boy has not awakened" (lōʾ hēqîṣ hannāʿar) — uses qîṣ (H6974 — awake) as a euphemism that assumes the possibility of wakening. The staff's failure emphasizes that the miracle requires the prophet himself, not a delegated implement.
4.32: hannāʿar mēt — "the child was dead." The same unambiguous māweṯ (H4191) used in v20. The scene is described in two clauses: Elisha enters the house, sees the child lying dead on the bed. The detail that it is "his bed" — the prophet's own bed in the upper room (v21) — is maintained. The dead child on the prophet's bed creates the narrative tension resolved by the miracle.
4.33: wayyitpallēl ʾel YHWH — "he prayed to YHWH." The verb pālal (H6419 — to pray, intercede) in the Hithpael appears here as the only explicit prayer in the entire miracle narrative. Elisha shuts the door (sāgar again, as in vv4-5), creating the private enclosure that recurs in Elisha's miracle-working. The prayer is not reported — only the fact of prayer. The miracle proceeds from the encounter with YHWH in private.
4.34: wayyighar ʿal hayyeleḏ — "he stretched himself upon the child." The verb gāḥar (H1457) is a hapax legomenon — it appears only here and in 1 Kgs 18:42, where Elijah crouches with his face between his knees on Carmel. The precise posture is uncertain; the description of mouth on mouth, eyes on eyes, hands on hands suggests a full-body contact covering of the child. ḥāmam (H2552 — be warm, get warm) describes the flesh warming: not restored to full life yet, but the process beginning.
4.35: wayyizōrēr hannāʿar šeḇaʿ pᵉʿāmîm — "the child sneezed seven times." The verb zārar (H2237 — to sneeze) is a hapax legomenon — it occurs only here in the entire Hebrew Bible, the unique biblical word for sneezing. The sevenfold sneezing (šeḇaʿ — the number of completeness) marks the full return of life: breath re-entering the body seven times. The final phrase — wayyipqaḥ ʿênāyw, "he opened his eyes" — uses pāqaḥ (H6491, to open eyes, to give sight), the same verb used for the opening of eyes at creation (Gen 3:5, 7) and healing (Isa 35:5; 42:7).
4.36: qᵉrāʾ laššûnammît hazzōʾt — "Call this Shunammite." Elisha summons her through Gehazi, maintaining the mediated relationship. His instruction to her — śᵉʾî binēḵ, "take up your son" — uses nāśāʾ (H5375 — lift, carry, take up), the verb of receiving and bearing a gift.
4.37: wayyiqqōd ʾarṣāh — "she bowed to the ground." The verb šāḥāh (H7812 — to bow down, prostrate oneself) expresses the deepest gesture of gratitude and honor; it is the same verb used for worship of YHWH and of kings. She falls at his feet, bows, and then — the final action — she takes up her son and goes out. The narrative resolution is complete: the child who was placed on the prophet's bed (v21) is now lifted (nāśāʾ) by his mother. The verbs of the crisis (put down, shut door) are reversed (take up, go out).
4.38: rāʿāb bāʾāreṣ — "a famine in the land." The famine (rāʿāb, H7458) connects to the broader pattern in Kings where prophetic ministry accompanies covenant crisis (Elijah's drought, 1 Kgs 17). wayyiṯbašal nāzîḏ — "make a stew/pottage" (nāzîḏ, H5138 — boiled food, pottage; the same word as Esau's stew in Gen 25:29). The sons of the prophets sit before Elisha (yōšᵉḇîm lᵉpānāyw) — the posture of disciples in formal instruction.
4.39: paqquʿōt śādeh — "wild gourds" (H6498 + H7704). The term paqquʿōt is identified by most scholars with Citrullus colocynthis (colocynth or bitter apple), a desert plant with extremely bitter, toxic fruit that causes violent intestinal distress. The gatherer is described as wᵉlōʾ yāḏaʿ — "not knowing what they were" — his ignorance is the narrative explanation for the crisis. The unknown toxin in the pot creates the prophetic problem of v40.
4.40: māweṯ bassîr — "death in the pot!" The cry uses māweṯ (H4191 — death) directly: they tasted it and immediately recognized the bitterness of the colocynth. The word sîr (H5518 — pot, caldron) appears three times in vv38-41 — the cooking vessel becomes the site of the miracle, as cooking vessels will again in vv42-44. wayyāḵlû is negated — they could not eat it.
4.41: qāḥ qāmaḥ — "bring flour" (qemaḥ, H7058 — flour, ground grain). The use of flour to neutralize the toxic stew has no natural explanation — flour does not neutralize colocynth bitterness. The action is a sign-act of prophetic authority: the prophet's word and the material action together accomplish what neither could accomplish separately. The narrator's evaluation — wᵉlōʾ hāyāh dāḇār rāʿ bassîr, "and there was no harmful thing in the pot" — is matter-of-fact; the miracle is stated without elaboration.
4.42: bikkûrîm — "firstfruits." The man from Baal-shalishah brings leḥem bikkûrîm (bread of firstfruits) and karmel (H3759 — fresh ears of grain, new grain). Bikkûrîm (H1061) is the technical term for the covenant offering of the first of the harvest (Exod 23:16; Lev 2:12-14; Num 18:13) — the gift of consecrated firstfruits to the prophet mirrors the dedicated offering of the first to YHWH. The gift's quantity — twenty barley loaves — is insufficient for a hundred men (v43), which is the point.
4.43: ʾettēn lîpnê meʾāh ʾîš — "set this before a hundred men?" The servant's objection restates the arithmetic problem (twenty loaves, one hundred men). Elisha's response — kōh ʾāmar YHWH ʾāḵōl wᵉhōtēr, "thus says YHWH: they shall eat and have some left over" — invokes the full authority formula of prophetic declaration: kōh ʾāmar YHWH (H3541 + H559 + H3068, "thus says YHWH"). The miracle is not just multiplication but the surplus that demonstrates the divine generosity exceeds exact sufficiency.
4.44: wayyōtēr kidᵉḇar YHWH — "and they had leftovers, according to the word of YHWH." The yōtēr (H3498 — remain, leave over) is the precise fulfillment of the prophetic word in v43. The formula kidᵉḇar YHWH (according to the word of YHWH) closes the miracle narrative with the standard fulfillment marker. The sequence — command, objection, divine word declared, fulfillment — is the structural prototype for the NT feeding miracles (Matt 14:15-21; John 6:7-13), which deliberately echo this scene.

5.1: gibbôr ḥayil wᵉhûʾ mᵉṣōrāʿ — "a mighty man of valor — and he had leprosy." The syntax places the military honorifics first (H1368 gibbôr, mighty warrior; H2428 ḥayil, valor/force) and the disease last, as a devastating qualifier: all the greatness ends with wᵉhûʾ mᵉṣōrāʿ ("but he was a leper"). The word ṣāraʿat (H6879) covers a range of skin diseases, not necessarily what modern medicine calls leprosy (Hansen's disease); it is the condition that rendered one ritually impure and excluded from community (Lev 13-14). The irony is the entire verse's point: the man through whom YHWH had given victory to Aram is himself unable to receive victory over his own flesh.
5.2: naʿărāh qᵉṭannāh — "a little girl." The double diminutive (naʿărāh, H5291 — young girl; qāṭān, H6996 — small, young) emphasizes her insignificance in social terms: a war captive, a household servant of a foreign general's wife. She has no name, no identity beyond her age and captive status. She is the unexpected source of the healing information — the paradigm of the lowly, unnamed instrument through whom divine provision flows. Her confidence — "he would cure him" — is stated in the conditional imperfect: yēʾāsēp (H622, gather/remove/take away), more literally "he would remove it from him."
5.3: The girl's speech uses the optative lûʾ (H3863 — would that/if only) + imperfect: "would that my lord were before the prophet." The conditional structure (ʾāz yēʾāsēp, "then he would remove") expresses confident hope, not doubt. mᵉṣōrāʿtô — "his leprosy" uses the Pual participle noun; the word becomes the organizing term of the entire chapter (occurs 7 times in ch. 5).
5.4: The chain of transmission — girl to mistress to Naaman to king — illustrates how divine initiative enters human political structures from the margins. The verb nāgad (H5046 — tell, report, declare) drives the narrative forward: it is used for both the girl's speech to the wife and Naaman's speech to the king. Information that originates with a captive child becomes a royal diplomatic initiative within two verses.
5.5: ʾiggeret ʾel melek yiśrāʾēl — "a letter to the king of Israel." The king of Aram reduces the prophetic healing to a royal transaction: šālaḥ sēper (send a letter). The gifts Naaman carries — ten talents of silver (kikkārîm), six thousand shekels of gold (šeqalîm), and ten changes of clothing (ḥᵃlîpōt bᵉgāḏîm) — are diplomatic tribute scaled to the request. The enormous wealth (roughly 10 talents = ~300 kg of silver; 6,000 shekels = ~70 kg of gold) reflects how much Naaman and the king of Aram are willing to spend.
5.6: The letter says wᵉʾāsaptā ʾet ṣāraʿtô — "you will remove/cure his leprosy." The same verb as v2 (ʾāsap, H622 — gather, take away). The letter addresses the king of Israel as if he is a healer — a fundamental category error the king immediately perceives (v7). The reduction of prophetic healing to royal transaction sets up the theological critique of the entire narrative.
5.7: hăʾĕlōhîm ʾānî lᵉhāmît ûlᵉhaḥᵃyôt — "Am I God, to kill and to make alive?" The king's theological insight is correct: the formulation māmît ûmᵉḥayyeh (H4191 + H2421 — kill and give life) is the exact language of Deut 32:39 ("I kill and I make alive") and 1 Sam 2:6 (Hannah's song). The king identifies the request as requiring divine power — he just fails to see that YHWH works through his prophet Elisha. His tearing of clothes (wayyiqraʿ bᵉgāḏāyw, H7167) signals the depth of perceived threat: this looks like a pretext for war.
5.8: yāḏaʿ kî yēš nāḇîʾ bᵉyiśrāʾēl — "let him know that there is a prophet in Israel." Elisha's intervention reframes the entire situation: the king's role is to redirect Naaman to the prophet, not to perform the healing himself. yāḏaʿ (H3045 — know) in the imperfect + kî frames the purpose of the prophetic demonstration: Naaman will know experientially that a prophet exists in Israel. This knowing is the narrative's goal before the theological confession of v15.
5.9: Naaman arrives with his military equipment — sûsāyw wᵉrikibbô (his horses and his chariot). He stops at the door of Elisha's house: pethah bayit ʾĕlîšāʿ. The juxtaposition of military pomp and a house door is deliberate. The great man with six thousand shekels of gold stands at a prophet's door — the social inversion the narrative is building toward.
5.10: lēḵ wᵉrāḥaṣtā šeḇaʿ pᵉʿāmîm bayardēn — "Go and wash (rāḥaṣ, H7364) in the Jordan seven times." Elisha sends a messenger, not himself. Two linguistic points: (1) rāḥaṣ (wash, bathe) is the verb in the instruction; ṭāḇal (dip, immerse) is the verb in v14 for what Naaman actually does — the difference between "wash yourself" and "immerse yourself" is significant; (2) yardēn (Jordan) is the covenant-crossing river, first named in relation to Israel's entry into the land (Josh 3-4). The instruction localizes the healing in the specific geography of covenant.
5.11: ʾāmar ʾānî ʾāmarti — "I thought to myself." The doubled speech verb (ʾāmar + ʾānî ʾāmarti, H559) marks Naaman's angry internal monologue. He expected: Elisha to come out (yāṣōʾ yēṣēʾ, imperfect emphatic — "he would surely come out"), to stand before him (wᵉʿāmaḏ), to call on the name of YHWH, and to wave his hand (wᵉhēnîp yāḏô, H5130 — wave, brandish) over the affected place. The expected ritual is dramatic, public, and involves direct contact — everything Elisha's actual instruction lacks. Naaman's expectations are precisely wrong.
5.12: hᵃlōʾ ṭôḇ... mikkōl mê yiśrāʾēl — "Are not [these rivers] better than all the waters of Israel?" ṭôḇ (H2895 — good, better) is the same evaluative term used throughout Genesis for creation. Naaman's argument is logical on its own terms: the rivers of Damascus (Abana/Amana and Pharpar) are large, clear rivers — demonstrably "better" as rivers than the Jordan, which is muddy and unremarkable. His question uses the same verb rāḥaṣ (wash) that Elisha used: "could I not wash in them and be clean?" The answer the narrative gives is: yes, but not for Naaman, not now, not for this purpose.
5.13: ʾāḇî — "my father." Naaman's servants address him with the term of intimate respect normally used for a father or a highly regarded elder. Their logic is irrefutable: if Elisha had commanded something difficult (dāḇār gāḏôl — a great/difficult word), Naaman would have done it. raq (H7535 — only, merely, just) emphasizes the smallness of the actual request: "how much more should you comply when he merely says: wash and be clean." The raq identifies Naaman's resistance as inversely proportional to the simplicity of the command.
5.14: wayyiṭbōl bᵉyardēn šeḇaʿ pᵉʿāmîm — "he dipped himself in the Jordan seven times." The verb here is ṭāḇal (H2881 — to dip, immerse, plunge into), not rāḥaṣ (wash) that Elisha commanded. Naaman does not merely wash but immerses. wᵉyāšāḇ bᵉśārô — "his flesh was restored" uses šûḇ (H7725 — return, turn back, restore) in the Qal imperfect: the flesh literally "returned" or "turned back" — not just healed but restored to its original condition. The comparison — kᵉḇaśar naʿar qāṭōn, "like the flesh of a little child" — uses the same phrase (naʿar qāṭōn) as the servant girl of v2. The little girl and the restored little-child flesh bracket the narrative.
5.15: yāḏaʿtî kî ʾên ʾĕlōhîm bᵉḵol hāʾāreṣ kî ʾim bᵉyiśrāʾēl — "I know that there is no God in all the earth except in Israel." The verb yāḏaʿ (H3045 — know) is now in the perfect: Naaman knows, has come to know. His monotheistic confession uses kî ʾim (H3588 + H518 — except, only) — the exclusive statement. His offer of a bᵉrāḵāh (H1293 — blessing, gift) to Elisha uses the covenant word for blessing; the "blessing" offered is the gift brought from Damascus. He presents what he brought as a gift of gratitude, using the beautiful ambiguity of the word that means both divine blessing and a material gift.
5.16: ḥay YHWH ʾăšer ʿāmadtî lᵉpānāyw — "As YHWH lives, before whom I stand." Elisha's refusal is sworn by the same oath formula used by Elijah (1 Kgs 17:1; 18:15). The phrase ʿāmadtî lᵉpānāyw (I stand before him) identifies Elisha as a courtier of YHWH — one who serves at the divine court. The refusal of the gift is theologically grounded: to accept payment would imply that the healing was a human service rather than a divine grace. wayyipṣar (H6484 — to urge strongly, press) — Naaman pressed him, but Elisha refused.
5.17: ʾădāmāh (H127 — ground, soil, earth) — two mule-loads of ʾădāmāh. The choice of ʾădāmāh rather than ʾereṣ (land, territory, H776) is significant: ʾădāmāh is the fertile, life-giving soil — the word of Gen 2:7 where YHWH forms the human from the ʾădāmāh. Naaman wants Israel's covenant soil to stand on when he worships YHWH. His theological reasoning — "your servant will offer burnt offering and sacrifice to no other god but YHWH" — reflects the ancient assumption that gods inhabit specific territories; his request is to bring Israel's sacred ground into Aram so that his worship of YHWH has a legitimate substrate.
5.18: sᵉlaḥ nā lᵉʿaḇdᵉḵā — "pardon your servant." The verb sālaḥ (H5545 — forgive, pardon) is the specific word for divine forgiveness, used almost exclusively with YHWH as subject in the OT (Num 14:19-20; 1 Kgs 8:30ff). Naaman directs this vocabulary to Elisha as mediator. The Rimmôn temple (H7417 — the Aramean storm god, cognate with Hadad) creates the political dilemma: Naaman must physically support the king when he bows in the pagan temple. His request — wᵉhištaḥᵃwîtî (and I bow down, H7812 — twice in this verse) — is honest about what he must do while declaring where his actual allegiance lies. The tension between bodily conformity and interior conviction is a genuine theological problem to which Elisha gives no rebuke.
5.19: lēḵ lᵉšālôm — "Go in peace." Elisha's send-off uses the standard Hebrew farewell formula but with the full weight of šālôm: wholeness, covenant completion. He makes no ruling on the Rimmon question; the silence is the answer. The brevity of his response — two words — after the theological complexity of vv17-18 is itself significant: the prophet does not legislate what Naaman must do in the Aramean court. The grace given is not conditional on perfect circumstances.
5.20: wayyōʾmer gēḥăzî naʿar ʾĕlîšāʿ — "Gehazi, the servant (naʿar) of Elisha, said to himself." The internal monologue (wᵉhûʾ nāʾamar, literally "and he said [to himself]") follows the pattern of Naaman's internal speech in v11. Gehazi's rationalization uses the same oath formula as Elisha in v16 (ḥay YHWH) — he swears by YHWH to commit a sin against YHWH's intent. rûṣ ʾaḥᵃrāyw (H7323 + H310 — run after him) — the verb of urgency that the Shunammite used in urgent faith (4:25) is here used in urgent greed.
5.21: Naaman sees Gehazi running after him (rāṣ ʾaḥᵃrāyw) and descends from his chariot — a sign of respect to Elisha's representative. The question hăšālôm — "is everything well?" — echoes the šālôm language of ch. 4. His response to what he perceives as the prophet's servant in legitimate service is warm and immediate.
5.22: šālôm — Gehazi's use of the peace-formula is the opposite of the Shunammite's: she used it as a statement of faith in adverse circumstances; he uses it as a deceptive opener for a fraudulent request. The lie is elaborate: two sons of the prophets, hill country of Ephraim, a talent of silver and two changes of clothing. The request is modest (compared to Naaman's original gift) — which makes it more plausible. Gehazi uses Elisha's name and authority (ʾǎdōnî šᵉlāḥanî, "my master has sent me") falsely.
5.23: hôʾel qah šᵉnê kikkārîm — "please accept two talents." Naaman insists on more than asked — he doubles the amount, pressing (pāraṣ, H6555 — urge, press, insist) Gehazi to take two talents. He binds the silver in two bags and hands them to his own servants to carry before Gehazi. The generous Gentile gives twice what the fraudulent Israelite requested.
5.24: At the hill (hāʿōpel, H6077 — a steep mound, specifically used of the citadel area in Samaria; cf. 2 Kgs 5:24 and Mic 1:5) Gehazi takes the goods from Naaman's servants and hides them in the house. The verb pāqad (H6485 — attend to, set in place) used here for storing the goods contrasts with the open exchange of the previous verses. The concealment is the basis for the lie to Elisha in v25.
5.25: ʾānāh hālaḵtā gēḥăzî — "Where did you go, Gehazi?" The question is a test — Elisha already knows (v26). Gehazi's denial — lōʾ hālaḵ ʿaḇdᵉḵā ʾānāh wᵉʾānāh, "your servant has not gone here or there" — uses the indefinite doubling of ʾānāh wᵉʾānāh (H575 × 2) for "anywhere at all." The absolute denial makes the lie complete.
5.26: lōʾ libbî hālaḵ ʾittᵉḵā — "Did not my heart go with you?" The noun lēḵ (from lāḵaḵ? No — this is lēḇ, H3820, heart/mind) — "was not my heart going with you?" — is a claim to prophetic awareness of Gehazi's journey. Elisha's heart (mind/perception) tracked Gehazi's movements in real time. The question hāʿēt lāqaḥat — "is this the time to take/receive?" — uses the ʿēt (H6256 — time, season, appointed time) of covenantal opportunity: there is a right time and a wrong time; this was the wrong time for a monetary transaction that would commercialize divine grace.
5.27: wᵉṣāraʿat naʿᵃmān tiḏbaq bᵉḵā — "the leprosy of Naaman will cling (dāḇaq, H1692) to you." The verb dāḇaq is the strongest Hebrew verb for adhesion, attachment, and covenant loyalty: it describes Ruth clinging to Naomi (Ruth 1:14), the covenant command to "hold fast" to YHWH (Deut 11:22; 13:5), and marital union (Gen 2:24). The irony is complete: the verb that describes unbreakable covenant devotion to YHWH is now used for the unbreakable punishment that will attend Gehazi and his descendants forever (lᵉʿôlām). What should have clung to him (covenant loyalty) did not; what should not cling to him (Naaman's leprosy) now will — forever. Gehazi goes out from Elisha's presence leprous, "white as snow" (mᵉṣōrāʿ kaššāleg) — the same snow-white appearance as Moses's temporary leprous condition (Exod 4:6) and Miriam's punishment (Num 12:10), but for Gehazi, permanent.
